title: Free-threading safety: lazy-init races, borrowed refs, unprotected per-instance dicts
author: devdanzin
body:
If you think investing in FT support is worth it, let me know and I'll open a PR or PRs.


Related to #345 (subinterpreter-safe refactor). [Full analysis report](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/be2bea00e879ed44e273eec1a0715f00).

## Context

We stress-tested `_speedups.c` (v3.20.2) under free-threaded Python 3.14.3t with TSan (17 scenarios, 4 threads × 200 iterations). The result was 0 races — but only because simplejson doesn't declare `Py_MOD_GIL_NOT_USED`, so CPython re-enables the GIL on import, serializing everything.

Static analysis with [ft-review-toolkit](https://github.com/devdanzin/ft-review-toolkit) (6 agents) identified the issues below. They are currently latent — they will surface if/when the module opts into free-threading.

The good news: simplejson's architecture is **favorable** for free-threading. Encoders and scanners are per-instance objects, so the common `simplejson.dumps()` / `simplejson.loads()` pattern (which creates fresh instances) is naturally thread-safe. The fixes are mechanical.

## Finding 1: 11 lazy-init TOCTOU races on function-scoped statics

11 `static PyObject *` variables are lazily initialized on first call using a non-atomic check-then-act pattern:

```c
static PyObject *s_null = NULL;
if (s_null == NULL) {
    s_null = JSON_InternFromString("null");
}
```

Two threads hitting this simultaneously both see `NULL`, both allocate, one result leaks. Under the C memory model this is undefined behavior.

| Variable | Function | Line |
|----------|----------|------|
| `s_null` | `_encoded_const` | 2700 |
| `s_true` | `_encoded_const` | 2710 |
| `s_false` | `_encoded_const` | 2720 |
| `open_dict` | `encoder_listencode_dict` | 2978 |
| `close_dict` | `encoder_listencode_dict` | 2985 |
| `empty_dict` | `encoder_listencode_dict` | 2992 |
| `open_array` | `encoder_listencode_list` | 3129 |
| `close_array` | `encoder_listencode_list` | 3136 |
| `empty_array` | `encoder_listencode_list` | 3143 |
| `sortargs` | `encoder_dict_iteritems` | 684 |
| `itemgetter0` | encoder init helper | 2631 |

**Fix**: Move all 11 to `init_constants()` / `moduleinit()`. They become write-once-read-many, matching the already-safe globals (`JSON_Infinity`, `JSON_NaN`, etc.). `itemgetter0` involves an import, so it should go in `moduleinit()` alongside the other `import_dependency` calls.

## Finding 2: 3 `PyDict_GetItem` borrowed-reference sites

`PyDict_GetItem` returns a borrowed reference that can be invalidated by concurrent dict mutation between the return and the subsequent `Py_INCREF`:

```c
memokey = PyDict_GetItem(s->memo, key);  // borrowed ref
if (memokey != NULL) {
    Py_INCREF(memokey);                  // race window: another thread mutates s->memo
    ...
```

| Dict | Function | Line |
|------|----------|------|
| `s->memo` | `_parse_object_str` | 1413 |
| `s->memo` | `_parse_object_unicode` | 1574 |
| `s->key_memo` | `encoder_listencode_dict` | 3072 |

**Fix**: Replace with `PyDict_GetItemRef` (CPython 3.13+, available via [pythoncapi-compat](https://github.com/python/pythoncapi-compat)):

```c
if (PyDict_GetItemRef(s->memo, key, &memokey) < 0)
    goto bail;
if (memokey != NULL) {
    // memokey is already a strong reference
    Py_DECREF(key);
    key = memokey;
}
```

Note: the existing `PyDict_GetItem` calls also suppress errors (see #348 "Additional CONSIDER items"). `PyDict_GetItemRef` fixes both the borrowed-ref and error-suppression issues.

## Finding 3: Unprotected per-instance dict mutations (shared-instance case)

If a single `JSONEncoder` or scanner is shared between threads (e.g., `encoder = simplejson.JSONEncoder(); pool.map(encoder.encode, items)`), the per-instance dicts race:

- **`s->markers`** (circular reference tracking): `PyDict_Contains` → `PyDict_SetItem` → `PyDict_DelItem` — check-then-act not atomic. Lines 2929-2964, 3007-3020, 3160-3173.
- **`s->key_memo`** (key string cache): `PyDict_GetItem` → `PyDict_SetItem` — read-then-write not atomic. Lines 3072-3081.
- **`s->memo`** (scanner key interning): `PyDict_GetItem` → `PyDict_SetItem`, plus `PyDict_Clear` after scanning. Lines 1413-1421, 1574-1581, 2362.

**Fix**: Add `Py_BEGIN_CRITICAL_SECTION(self)` / `Py_END_CRITICAL_SECTION()` to `scanner_call` (line 2340) and `encoder_call` (line 2679). Since all mutable state access flows through these entry points, a single critical section at the top serializes per-object access:

```c
static PyObject *
encoder_call(PyEncoderObject *self, PyObject *args, PyObject *kwds)
{
    Py_BEGIN_CRITICAL_SECTION(self);
    // ... existing body ...
    Py_END_CRITICAL_SECTION();
    return result;
}
```

## Migration path

1. Move 11 lazy-init statics to `init_constants()` / `moduleinit()` (Finding 1)
2. Replace 3 `PyDict_GetItem` with `PyDict_GetItemRef` (Finding 2)
3. Add `Py_BEGIN_CRITICAL_SECTION` to `scanner_call` and `encoder_call` (Finding 3)
4. Add `Py_MOD_GIL_NOT_USED` (only after 1-3)
5. Multi-phase init + heap types + per-module state (extends #345)

Steps 1-3 are the safety-critical work. Step 5 is the structural migration tracked in #345.

<sub>Found using [ft-review-toolkit](https://github.com/devdanzin/ft-review-toolkit). This issue was drafted by Claude Code and reviewed by a human.</sub>

comments.author: etrepum
comments.body: I do think that supporting free-threading and subinterpreters is a worthwhile task, would be happy to review PRs implementing this feature

comments.author: etrepum
comments.body: I think all of these are resolved?
